coredata.dc:description: The size range of an atom is approximately 3–5 Å (10−10 m), while a nanometer is 10−9 m. Thus, nanomaterials are used as multipliers that can be used to attach several single atoms to the surface. It is similar to an exquisitely-arranged production line, with every single atom acting as an operator, working on each catalytic reaction. As applied to biological applications, these catalytic targets become proteins in vivo, and the transformation of nanomaterials into single-atom catalytic enzymes illustrates the potential of the new single-atom nanozyme (SAN) nanotechnology for biological applications. As SAN materials on the order of 1 nm–100 nm are selected as carriers, such as carbon quantum dots and metal-organic frameworks, they can load single atoms and make their reaction efficiency much higher than that of regular enzymes. Moreover, the support materials have the optical property of absorbing the radiation that allows the nanocomposite to influence the behavior of the chemical reactions. In this review, we present novel SANs that can both increase the catalytic rate and produce diagnostic and therapeutic effects.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Biochemical applications of SANs materials. They can be used in cancer therapy, oxidative stress reduction, bacterial infection inhibition, and as bio-sensors.

Evolution, synthesis, and demonstration of SANs materials. (a) The process of single-atomic material preparation extends from bulk to nano-dimension and atomization. (b) Synthesis of SANs materials. (c) The single-atom structure is demonstrated by density functional theory. However, although density functional theory has many applications, two shortcomings must be remedied.

Crystal structure and optical characterization of SANs materials. (a) X-ray absorption and (b) backscattering. Optical properties of nano support quantum dots. (c) Kassa rule and (d) solvent quenching effect.

The active atomic sites of SANs were designed to obtain better catalytic properties. (a) Schematic illustration of Fe-based SANs for photoelectrochemical sensing mechanism [59]. (b) Calibration plots of steady-state current for dopamine and uric acid [63]. (c) Schematic illustration of the photoelectrochemical biosensor using SANs [92]. (d) Scheme of colorimetric sensing platform for malathion assay based on Fe-based SANs [60]. Copyright 2012, American Chemical Society; 2020, 2021, and 2022 Elsevier.

The POD-like mimicking SANs were designed for the biosensing field. (a) Schematic representation of lateral flow immunoassay for simultaneous detection based on Fe–N–C SANs with signal amplification [61]. (b) Schematic illustration of the SANs system for organophosphorus pesticide detection [77]. (c) Schematic illustration of the Co-Based SANs for chemiluminescent immunoassay protocol [81]. (d) Schematic illustration of carcinoembryonic antigen detection using the prepared SANs [62]. (e) Schematic Illustration of Fe-based SANs nanoprobe synthesis and hydrogen peroxide detection [69]. Copyright 2022, Elsevier; 2020, 2021, and 2022 American Chemical Society; 2021 Springer Nature.

CAT-like and SOD-like SANs are designed to assist in the healing process. (a) Schematic illustration of SANs nanoprobe with iron atoms were immobilized on N-doped porous carbon to complete the mimicry of SOD and CAT [93]. (b) SANs have a better catalytic effect than nanozymes [78]. (c) A schematic illustration of N-doped carbon was constructed to support Fe–Cu, Rh, and V to form SANs [42]. Copyright 2021, The Royal Society of Chemistry; 2020, Wiley-VCH GmbH; 2022 Springer Nature.

SANs can be used to prevent injury from irritations and are anti-bacterial. (a) and (b) Schematic illustration of Fe-based SANs for anti-bacterial [43,45]. (c) Schematic illustration of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection treatment with Cu-based SANs [73]. (d) Schematic illustration of the Fabrication Process of the SANs-modified Cotton [71]. (e) Schematic illustration of the process of W-based SANs resistance to biological pollution and uranium extraction from seawater [70]. Copyright 2019, WILEY-VCH GmbH; 2021 and 2022 Elsevier; 2021 American Chemical Society.

Different materials are designed for the SANs building for cancer treatment. (a) Schematic illustration of SANs applied for PTT and catalysis [47]. (b) Schematic illustration of the preparation of Fe-CDs SANs and the initiation of an enzymatic cascade to regulate ROS to treat GBM [51]. (c) Schematic illustration of osteosarcoma elimination enabled by Fe-based bioglass scaffold [53]. (d) Schematic illustration of the working mechanisms of platelet membrane-coated Fe-based SANs for PTT [49]. (e) Schematic Illustration of SANs for selective ferroptosis [52]. (f) FeN3P-centred SANs demonstrate POD-like catalytic activity [54]. Copyright 2021 and 2022, Springer Nature; 2022, Elsevier; 2021, Wiley-VCH GmbH; 2022, American Chemical Society.
Literature related to SANs published in the last five years. The types of metals studied, active sites, catalytic properties, and applications are listed in the table.

head.ce:title: Tunable single-atom nanozyme catalytic system for biological applications of therapy and diagnosis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The size range of an atom is approximately 3–5 Å (10−10 m), while a nanometer is 10−9 m. Thus, nanomaterials are used as multipliers that can be used to attach several single atoms to the surface. It is similar to an exquisitely-arranged production line, with every single atom acting as an operator, working on each catalytic reaction. As applied to biological applications, these catalytic targets become proteins in vivo, and the transformation of nanomaterials into single-atom catalytic enzymes illustrates the potential of the new single-atom nanozyme (SAN) nanotechnology for biological applications. As SAN materials on the order of 1 nm–100 nm are selected as carriers, such as carbon quantum dots and metal-organic frameworks, they can load single atoms and make their reaction efficiency much higher than that of regular enzymes. Moreover, the support materials have the optical property of absorbing the radiation that allows the nanocomposite to influence the behavior of the chemical reactions. In this review, we present novel SANs that can both increase the catalytic rate and produce diagnostic and therapeutic effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the subnano field has been widely commercialized, the single-atom area is becoming a research target for scientists to further break the limits of catalytic efficiency. Monoatomic chemistry is a concept that has been actively investigated by scientists in the last two decades and is becoming a front-end scientific field. The basic idea can be traced back to 1925, when Professor Taylor's team revealed the concept of catalytically active centers. In the 1960s, Prof. Michel Boudart's team developed the concepts of metal dispersion and structure sensitivity. The first single-atom catalysts were defined in 2011 by Academician Tao Zhang of the Dalian Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Professor Jun Li of Tsinghua University, and Professor Jing-Yue Liu of Arizona State University as carrier-type catalysts in which all metal components are dispersed in the form of single atoms [1].

In addition, the history of the development of monatomic gold has essential vital points. Yuan et al. synthesized Au6 on platinum to expand the field of the subnanometer level, and Malta et al. [2] successfully synthesized monatomic gold containing chlorine coordination by using carbon powder as a carrier in an acidic solid aqueous solution. Chen et al. [3] used the trinitrogen atoms of ligands to immobilize monoatomic gold, resulting in an oxidation number of +1 for monoatomic gold. In industry, the research goal is the pursuit of low cost and high productivity, among which is the reduction of precious metal consumption. Nanoparticles have excellent catalytic properties, but they are still unable to overcome this limitation. Due to the unique structure of monoatomic catalysts, they can be regarded as intermediates between nonhomogeneous and homogeneous catalysts, further breaking the limits of activity, selectivity, and stability of nanoparticles. Single-atom catalysts can be divided into the carrier and metallic single-atom parts. First, the monatomic catalysts can be designed as carriers with high dispersion regarding the expected reaction environment so that the single-atom catalysts are close to the homogeneous catalysts and increase the molecular collision rate. In addition, a suitable coordination environment was provided to enhance the interaction force between the metal and the carrier so that the metal exhibits a nonintegral average oxidation number, which can accelerate the catalytic reaction rate by a synergistic effect. In monoatomic metals, highly dispersed metals with a high specific surface area are the activation center of the reaction. This level of dispersion can prevent the metal of nanoparticles from being encapsulated and unable to carry out catalysis, allowing the overall catalyst atom use efficiency to be significantly increased. In addition, the monatomic metal has no crystalline surface or site phase, effectively avoiding side reactions or toxicity during the catalytic reaction. In terms of stability, for carriers with lattice defects, single metal atoms can be embedded in the defective sites to reduce metal loss, as evidenced by the long catalytic cycle [3–5]. In this review, we summarize the basic properties of single-atom nanozymes (SANs or SAzymes), emphasizing the emerging applications that take advantage of these multiple biological applications. SANs are a new concept in multiphase catalysis, where atomically dispersed homogeneous active sites maximize not only the utilization of metal atoms but also have the potential to bridge the gap between multiphase and homogeneous catalysis. SANs do not mean that a single zero-valent metal atom is the active center. SANs also have coordination interactions with other atoms of the carrier, such as electron transfer, and often exhibit a certain degree of charge, which is the main reason for the high activity of catalysts. The unique catalytic ability of SANs can be applied in cancer therapy, reduction of oxidative stress, inhibition of bacterial infection, and as bio-sensors, as shown in Fig. 1 . This review is of considerable interest to studies with other single-atom catalysis and has tried to discover the possibility of non-invasive precision medicine in the future.

Since technology advanced sufficiently to observe and manipulate the nanoscale structure of matter, much research has been conducted on the properties of materials at the nanoscale. At this scale, many physicochemical properties have been discovered that are very different from those of macroscopic materials. These properties, which evolve with the material scale, include electrical conductivity and magnetic, optical, and mechanical properties. One of the most fascinating is the catalytic properties of nanomaterials, and nanogold catalysts are the most representative. The discovery and use of catalysts are more than 150 years old, and the German chemist Wilhelm Ostwald has provided the most widely accepted definition of a catalyst to date: a catalyst can accelerate a chemical reaction without becoming part of the final product. In other words, the catalyst can form an intermediate product with the reacting molecules to provide another rapid pathway for the reaction, after which the catalyst returns to its original state. Currently, catalysts are used in a wide range of applications. In environmental protection, catalysts are needed to treat many pollutants, such as automobile exhaust and factory waste gas. For the development of new energy sources, such as fuel cells, catalysts are required to catalyze the oxidation of fuels to produce electricity. The global market demand for catalysts used in the automotive and petrochemical industries exceeds US$100 billion annually and is increasing rapidly. Understanding the catalytic mechanism and designing new catalysts to optimize their function have become essential issues for scientists.

Catalysis can be classified into “homogeneous catalysis” and “heterogeneous catalysis,” which indicate whether the catalyst and the reactant are in the same phase, such as the gas or liquid phase. Heterogeneous catalysis, in which solid catalysts react with gaseous or liquid molecules, accounts for the largest share. Most solid catalysts are composed of a stator, that is, the carrier of the catalyst, and metal particles. Early catalytic developments were more like goldsmithing. Although the fundamental mechanisms and details were not well understood, much progress was made through experimentation and formulation of various combinations, such as the Haber-Bosch process, better known for manufacturing ammonia in the early 20th century. Fritz Haber (1909) and later Carl Bosch (1913) developed iron-based catalysts mixed with other metals, and ammonia was formed when heated nitrogen and hydrogen passed through these catalysts. The discovery of gold nanocatalysts has inspired research on the primary mechanism of catalysis, and its practical applications have been widely explored. It is also expected that it can be used as a carbon monoxide sensor and to participate in the operation of fuel cells to oxidize carbon monoxide and reduce the interference of carbon monoxide with fuel reactions. International catalyst companies are also seriously investigating various industrial applications for gold nanocatalysts. The greatest challenge for these applications is the tendency of the gold nanoparticles on the support to aggregate to form larger nanoparticles under heated conditions and when exposed to various reaction gases. This aggregation phenomenon is common to all types of metal catalysts and is a natural tendency of nanoparticles. This aggregation results in a smaller surface area and lower catalytic yields. However, the situation is very different for gold nanoparticles because their catalytic properties are derived from their particular nanometer size. Once this size is changed by aggregation, the catalytic ability of gold nanoparticles will be lost entirely. Therefore, the first and foremost task in applying gold nanocatalysts is overcoming the aggregation of gold nanoparticles in the reaction. Studying the interaction between various stents and gold nanoparticles is essential, as developing different techniques enhances the interaction to combat the aggregation phenomenon. The most suitable way to solve the problem of metallic materials that cause aggregation and lead to ineffectiveness is to prepare single-atom catalytic nanomaterials.

Single-atom catalysis is a new concept in multiphase catalysis, where atomically dispersed homogeneous active sites maximize not only the utilization of metal atoms but also have the potential to bridge the gap between multiphase and homogeneous catalysis. Single-atom catalysts do not mean that a single zero-valent metal atom is the active center. Single-atom catalysts also have coordination interactions with other atoms of the carrier, such as electron transfer, and often exhibit a certain degree of charge, which is the main reason for the high activity of catalysts. The synthesis of novel single-atom catalysts is becoming increasingly abundant, and their applications are becoming more widespread. However, there is still much room for the development of single-atom catalysts, such as highly stable, highly active, and highly loaded single-atom catalysts, which still need to be explored to realize real industrial applications; at the same time, the crossover with various metallic organic homogeneous catalysts and enzyme catalysis is not deep; there is also the synthesis and application of precisely controllable diatomic and triatomic cluster catalysts and the precise regulation of single-atom coordination to realize highly active and single-active catalysts. The synthesis of single active sites, such as homogeneous MN4 catalysts, shows that the road to single-atom catalysis is long, and the future is promising. The preparation and identification of single-atom catalytic materials are indispensable for research in this field; therefore, the following paragraphs will first introduce how to obtain SANs and their testing methods.

The standard single-atom synthesis methods are impregnation and coprecipitation, defect design, atomic layer deposition, and coordination site design, as shown in Fig. 2 b. Li et al. [6] used this method to encapsulate monoatomic platinum in deionized water with carbide. Ji et al. [7] used this method to inlay monatomic platinum onto molybdenum carbide and molybdenum nitride to compare the difference in metal properties of the carriers. In the second type of defect design, this method is characterized by an intermetallic solid carrier force to fix the metal atoms, effectively solving the problem of atomic leakage. However, the distribution and regulation of defects are still significant challenges. The lattice defects can be divided into lattice defects and carbon defect environments; the lattice defects of oxygen are generally used as coordination to fix metal atoms, while the carbon defect environment is applied to metals with strong bonding to nitrogen or carbon. Cao et al. [8] synthesized single-atom gold in titanium dioxide in an alkaline solution. Zhang et al. [9] sintered graphite with nickel hydroxide at high temperatures and immobilized single atoms in graphite defects. The atomic layer deposition method is currently the most widely used monatomic technique in industry, where single-layer atoms are deposited on a carrier by mechanical spraying. The impurities are removed and bonded to platinum to protect the surface. Finally, the ligand environment is designed for polymers that often contain contaminants such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur, which can be ligated with monatomic metals by strong ligand forces with high design flexibility and relatively stable ligand environments. Liu et al. [10] used FeCl2·4H2O and CoCl2·6H2O as precursors and successfully synthesized iron monoatomic and cobalt atoms coordinated by four nitrogen atoms after sintering at high temperatures to enhance the catalytic efficiency through the synergistic effect of dual monoatomic interactions. The monatomic metal was immobilized by the organometallic framework, and the nitrogen atom was immobilized by the coordination environment.

As the basic research of nanotechnology becomes more and more complete, its application in the field of biomedical science also evolves. From prevention and diagnosis to the treatment of diseases, there is a role for nanotechnology to play. In addition to using the properties of nanocarriers themselves to enhance the efficacy of drugs, other technologies can be used instead to overcome the obstacles in the field of drug development. The toxicity of supports is considered the main risk due to the neglectable harm of atomic dispersion of active metals. Therefore, the materials of choice are high stability, multi-functional, primarily biodegradable, nontoxic carbon-based, and nontoxic silicon-based materials.

Carbon quantum dots are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with luminescence properties. They are quasi-zero dimensional with a rich source of raw materials, easy surface modification, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence wavelength, and wide two-photon absorption area [11]. In general, the term carbon dots refers to one of the types of carbon materials, such as graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, polymer dots, and carbonized polymer dots, all of which are similar materials [12]. The size of carbon quantum dots should be less than 10 nm, and the carbon nuclei are mainly composed of sp
2 and sp
3 orbital domains with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface, making the carbon quantum dots highly hydrophilic and biocompatible [13]. Graphene quantum dots are nanoscale graphene stacks, generally smaller than five layers, with nitrogen or oxygen-containing functional groups at the edges. The planar direction size of a graphene quantum dot is larger than its thickness and it has anisotropic properties [14]. Carbon nanodots are very close to carbon quantum dots. Nevertheless, they are highly carbonized structures, mainly composed of sp
3 hybrid orbital domains, with no obvious lattice structure and up to 100 nm in diameter. The most characteristic feature of polymer dots is that they are attached to the surface and have several functional groups, cross-linkages, and high dispersion. These properties enhance the luminescence efficiency of the fluorescent molecules on the polymer [15]. Carbonized polymer dots are carbonized products of polymer dots, which not only maintain the properties of the polymer but also have higher nitrogen and oxygen contents, high hydrophilicity, and high luminescence of the carbon nuclei. They can be considered intermediate products between carbon nanodots and polymer dots [16,17]. According to the above properties, these types of carbon dots are not only easily to be metabolized but improve the catalytic effect of active sites. Their application can be extended after doing surface modification.

Silicon dioxide (SiO2) is an acidic oxide that corresponds to silicic acid's aqueous compound (H2SiO3). SiO2 is the most prevalent and important compound of silicon. The natural silica on Earth accounts for approximately 12% of the mass of the Earth's crust and exists in two primary forms, crystalline and amorphous, collectively known as silica. The reticulated structure of silica determines its excellent physical and chemical properties. Because of its widespread existence in nature, silica has been widely used by humans from ancient times to the present. Silicon dioxide is melted with other compounds at high temperatures and cooled quickly to produce glass. It is currently one of the primary raw materials used in semiconductor, solar panel, and biomedical applications. Nanosilica is an odorless, nontoxic, and nonpolluting nonmetallic material. Its structure is primarily a reticulated particle structure, and the basic particle form is spherical. Silicon dioxide crystals are all based on silicon atoms as the core, tetrahedrally connected to four oxygen atoms. Each oxygen atom is bonded to two silicon atoms to form a compound with a reticular structure. Nano silicon dioxide is highly reactive and can bring unique properties, such as easy surface modification of silanol groups, optical shielding properties, and chemical stability. Silica does not react with acids, alkalis, reductants, or oxidizers at room temperature. Generally, the solution for dissolving the silica structure is mainly hydrofluoric acid (HF), and the reaction between silica and hydrofluoric acid forms SiF4. Nanosilica is an inorganic powder material that can be produced in large quantities and at low cost. It is currently used in silicone rubber, organic compounds, coatings, biopharmaceuticals, cosmetics, inks, and electronic materials. Even though there are no obvious optical properties in silica series, the robust structure provides us a chance to synthesize various morphology. Hereafter, the silica supports turn into platforms to contain other single-atom nanoparticles or become high-surface area single-atom catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active sites with candidate metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The key to the design of SANs depends on the biotoxicity of the nanosupport and the metal, both of which must be biocompatible materials. Transition metals easily coordinate with the nanosupport due to their abundant d orbital domains. In particular, the redox-active ability of transition metals can disrupt redox homeostasis in tumor cells. The candidate metals can be applied to different nanosupport systems to fine-tune the enzyme-like activity by the designed coordination structures. Five common active site systems of SANs are described below.

Rh, V, Fe, Cu, and Fe–Cu are studied, coordinated with four or six nitrogen atoms. High peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT)-like functions are performed by RhN4 and VN4 SANs. The reaction energy barriers can be decreased under the active center of circular catalytic processes in the Rh/V–O–N4 structure. The unique module of RhN4 and VN4 linked with two-sided oxygen can result in high catalytic efficiency. When these metals were replaced with Fe–Cu, superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity was observed. Otherwise, VN4 possessed high GPx-like activity. Based on the DFT calculation, five steps are proposed: (1) H2O2 formation, (2) bilateral adsorption, (3) oxygen release, (4) superoxide ion restock, and (5) H2O2 release. The SANs of Rh/VN4 followed the step 2 → 1 → 5 → 4 → 3; Fe–Cu–N6 followed with 1 → 2 → 4 → 3 → 5; FeN4 and CuN4 followed by 2 → 1 → 3 → 5 [18].

Many natural enzymes can be observed in which P species are essential components and assist electron tunneling to the SANs’ active center. When the P species are added to the precursor, they improve anchoring of the metal active center and the local coordination structure. After P doping, the specific activity values were 30-fold and 10-fold higher than those of Fe3O4 SAN and FeN3P SAN, respectively. Lower barriers for O/OH formation resulted in the higher POD-like catalytic activity of FeN3P SAN (<0.5 eV). OH species cannot be easily converted into H2O (1.05 eV) under the FeN4 SAN catalytic process. The active center of the Fe atom will be bound by OH, inhibiting the whole catalytic process. According to the analysis, the Fe center presents a less positive charge. The electron donation of P atoms can be confirmed with the higher catalytic activity of FeN3P SAN under the synergistic effect of Fe and P atoms [19,20].

The electronic structure of the central metal atom can be tuned under heteroatom doping. After boron (B), with its lower electronegativity, is doped into the SAN system, the electron density and electronic structure of the central atom can be regulated. Taking Zn SAN as an example, the dispersibility and hydrophilicity of Zn SANs are improved after doping with B atoms. This doping also enhanced the catalytic performance due to the promotion of the accessibility of the active sites to the solution [21,22].

Oxide-based SANs, such as TiO2 and CeO2, provide another nanosupport for the design of SAN. The number of defects is a crucial factor affecting the local electron density of the SAN active center. Co metal ions are dispersed in a nanoporous TiO2 hollow sphere and treated under air or nitrogen to observe the difference in the activity and defects of a SAN. The defect generation of Co SANs under a nitrogen atmosphere is higher than that under an air atmosphere because oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ defects preferentially occur under a nitrogen atmosphere. According to the Extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) analysis, the weak interaction between Co and Ti atoms under the air atmosphere results in less charge transformation. The SANs under an air atmosphere possess a higher content of Co2+ with better enzyme activity. The SANs with better activity can be designed with more transition metal ions and fewer oxygen vacancies [23–25].

Unlike natural enzymes, the catalytic process of SANs first undergoes substrate adsorption, followed by surface reactions that lead to product dissociation and regeneration of the active sites on the surface. Therefore, the catalytic process of SANs is closely related to their surface structure. Meanwhile, since SANs are mostly inorganic nanoparticles or carbon-based or silicon-based materials containing metal elements, their biostability, biocompatibility, dispersibility, and targeting need to be improved for biomedical applications. For this purpose, the best choice is to modify the surface so SANs can be effectively dispersed in biomimetic fluids. For example, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is often used to improve the hydrophilicity and biocompatibility of the material. The PEG molecules prevent SANs from interacting with biological fluid components, thereby reducing the clearance of mononuclear phagocytosis and prolonging the duration of SANs in blood circulation. In addition to PEG, polyvinylpyrrolidone is often used as a surface modifier to improve the dispersibility of SANs. Surface modification of SANs can further functionalize and enhance in vivo/in vitro bioaccumulation, which can provide SANs with the ability to facilitate bacterial/cancer therapy, multimodal imaging, and tumor targeting. Muhammad et al. [26] modified the specific Angiopep-2 on the surface of Fe-bases SANs. Angiopep-2 is a novel peptide that specifically recognizes the highly expressed LRP-1 receptor in glioblastoma cells and can be transported in the central nervous system for brain tumor targeting, crossing the blood-brain barrier and ensuring the selective accumulation of SANs in glioma cells through specific interactions. According to the results of Muhammad et al., they demonstrated that angiopep-2 functionalized Fe-CDs SANs exhibited significantly higher cellular uptake in U87MG cells than their unmodified counterparts. In addition to molecular modification, cell membrane coating is also widely used for the surface modification of SANs. Liu et al. [27] used cancer cell membrane (CM) to coat Fe-based SANs and synthesized Fe-based SAN@CM. This prolongs the circulation time of SANs and enhances tumor targeting. From the above findings, it can be found that modulating the activity of SANs by surface modification has established a successful strategic approach in the field of nanomedicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of physical and chemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the advantages of monoatomic nanofermentation, the high atomic efficiency of monoatomic nanofermentation was analyzed and tested, supplemented by density functional theory calculations to establish the critical factors of high efficiency and the simulated structure of monoatomic nanofermentation. In addition, optical testing of synchrotron radiation identification helps to understand the bioavailability of substrate materials. Only then can the monoatomic hybrid SANs be applied in cellular and animal experiments to demonstrate the efficacy of cancer therapy.

Density functional theory is widely used in computational calculations, mainly in solid-state physics and molecular gas simulations. The calculation of ground states is the main area of research, and optimization and simplification are used to simulate accurate ground states. However, multiple structural systems and hybrid functions make the calculation difficult, and the energy band difference between the excited and groundstates is not easy to find. Therefore, to improve the accuracy, scientists have designed various equations, such as MN15, MN15-L, revM11, and M11plus. At the same time, a database is established annually to promote the expansion of theoretical and computational resources [28]. Although density generalized function theory has a wide range of applications, it has two major defects that need to be remedied, as shown in Fig. 2c [29]. These are delocalization error and fractional charges; and static correlation error and fractional spins. Using linear energy as a function of fractional charge will lead to delocalization error. The conservation of energy causes the error of static correlation error being a function of fractional spin. In recent years, scientists have used various simulation equations to reduce these errors and extend the limits of this theoretical calculation. The VASP software package is based on density generalization theory [30,31]. The projector augmented-wave method addresses the interaction between valence electrons and ion cores [32]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof exchange-correlation generalization describes the exchange-correlation interaction between valence electrons based on the generalized gradient approximation [33]. The structural relaxation, which covers both atomic coordinates and lattice parameters, can be calculated by the conjugate gradient method, where the system energy of the structural relaxation is related to the atomic energy of the atoms subjected to the gradient. The plane wave cutoff energy is set to 400 eV, and the Brillouin zone is sampled at a kmesh of 3 × 3 × 1 for the system involving Au (111) surfaces. The Brillouin zone was sampled using gamma points for the carbon quantum dot system, and the vacuum layer thickness was set to 2 nm. Through integrating the above simulation functions, we can verify the most possible structure. In advance, the reaction pathway and electronic structures are also be revealed to backup the experimental details.

In 1913 using salt crystal experiments, Bragg et al. found that regularly arranged crystals would produce regular scattering of X-rays. Suppose the lattice spacing is an integer multiple of the wavelength of the incident light. In that case, the diffracted light will undergo constructive interference to enhance the diffraction phenomenon, and the spectrum will appear brightly patterned. Conversely, if the lattice spacing is a noninteger multiple of the wavelength of the diffracted light, the scattered light is weakened by destructive interference, and the spectrum appears dark. This phenomenon satisfies Bragg's Law, as shown in equations (3)–(1). The variable d is the lattice spacing between adjacent crystal planes, θ is the angle between the incident light and the crystal plane, λ is the wavelength of the diffracted light, and hkl is the Miller index.
(1)
2 d h k l
sin
θ = n λ

The single crystal phase has a highly ordered crystal plane, and a narrow peak with high relative intensity can be detected when it satisfies the crystal plane of Bragg diffraction. The amorphous phase has only short-range order, and the detected spectrum is broadband. By referring to the standard spectrum, the purity and structural information of the sample can be judged. Synchrotron radiation is a type of powerful light source in the XRD field. The highly intensive and high-coherent light source is suitable to collect higher resolution data than that of the in-house one. For example, in National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC), the working wavelength of the 01C2 beamline is 0.82657 Å. Using the MAR345 image disk as a detector, we can obtain a clear carbon quantum dot XRD spectrum in less than 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spherical aberration corrected transmission electron microscopy (AC-TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of basic structure, spherical aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy is not much different from traditional transmission electron microscopy. However, the resolution of the conventional transmission electron microscope is limited by aberrations, and the anomalies can be divided into 1. Diffraction aberration due to limitations of physical optics. Second, spherical aberration (spherical aberration) is attributable to the aberration caused by the objective lens. Third, astigmatism aberration (astigmatism) is due to the asymmetry of the magnetic field of the objective lens. Fourth, wavelength dispersion aberration (chromatic aberration) is attributable to the wavelength change of electrons. Spherical aberration correction Scanning transmission electron microscopy can solve spherical aberration, astigmatic aberration, and wavelength dispersion aberration with a spherical aberration corrector. The complex, strong magnetic field is mainly provided by the corrector's quadrupole magnet, hexapole magnet, and octupole magnet. The electron beam can be focused by adjusting the magnetic field's positive and negative deviation on the plane's magnetic field. Scanning transmission electron microscopy combines transmission-type and scanning-type functions. The thickness of the sample needs to be less than 100 nm, and the image is presented as a grayscale. It can solve the signal problem of low spatial correlation in general electron microscopes, improve spatial resolution and provide atomic-level structural information. AC-TEM installed with a cold field emission electron gun provides samples in stable condition. The accelerating voltage can be adjusted to 200 kV or 80 kV depending on the user's samples. Owing to the above advantages, we can catch the images of single-atom nanomaterials and check the atomic dispersion by AC-TEM.

The most basic emission mechanism of quantum dots is the Kasha rule, and other influencing factors are the solvatochromic shift, quantum confinement effect, surface states and defects, and passivation agent. Kasha's rule was published by Mike Kasha in 1950, and it is defined as the lowest excitation energy level of the multiplet at the emission energy level of an electron multiplet [34]. When an electron absorbs photons from the ground state to an excited state, the excited electron can only emit photons from the lowest excited state; that is, the emission wavelength is independent of the excitation light wavelength In 2003, Borrelli et al. [35] established a theoretical calculation model with the help of the Franck-Condon factor and the Hamiltonian matrix, considering the influence of all vibrational energy levels when the vibrational coordinates overlap and the coordinates are highly replaced. This method suggests that rapid internal conversion is the main reason for Kasha's rule. Solvation discoloration is the emission mechanism of quantum dots in the solution state. The absorption half-life is approximately 10−15 s, and the emission half-life is approximately 10−9 s. According to Kasha's rule, the relationship between the absorption wavelength and the emission wavelength is independent of each other. However, when the carbon quantum dots are dispersed in a polar solvent, the solvent molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the carbon quantum dots, which changes the emission mechanism and produces three situations [36]. When the half-life of solvation relief is much longer than the radiation half-life, it is not affected by solvation and maintains the original emission mechanism. When the half-life of solvation relief is much shorter than the half-life of radiation, due to the quenching of solvation, no photons are emitted at the energy level of carbon quantum dots, and light is emitted at the energy level of solvent molecules in the form of homogeneous broadening. When the half-life of solvation relief is equal to the half-life of emission, the emission wavelength is redshifted with the absorption wavelength, called inhomogeneous broadening. At this time, emission occurs at the carbon quantum dot and solvent energy levels. In a nonpolar solvent, there is no solvation quenching effect because there is no solvent molecule adsorption, as shown in Fig. 3 a.

The quantum confinement effect is used in quantum mechanics to quantify energy, and the energy level is considered discontinuous. When the particle radius is smaller than its Bohr radius, the band gap will decrease as the particle radius increases; the redshift of the emission wavelength is known as the quantum confinement effect. The calculation of the optical energy gap is shown in formula (3-2).
(2)
E g
o p t
=
h c
e
λ
e d g e
where opt is the optical energy gap, h is Planck's constant (6.626 × 10−34 m2kgs−1), e is the electron charge (1.6 × 10−19 coulombs), c is the speed of light in vacuum (3 × 108 m s−1), and λedge is the wavelength of the highest absorption edge. When the diameter of the quantum dot increases, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital both show a downward trend, resulting in a decrease in the optical energy gap and a redshift in the emission wavelength.

When the carbon quantum dots are excited, the valence band electrons are stimulated to transition to the conduction band, the valence band loses electrons to generate holes, and the conduction band gains electrons to form active electrons, which are relieved by internal radiation to emit photons. In the photoexcitation spectrometer, a detector is installed at the vertical position of the excitation light source, which dramatically reduces the noise caused by the light source and can obtain accurate detection wavelengths. This is the concept of photoluminescence spectroscopy. In addition, by giving pulsed laser excitation light, a photon counter is used to measure the life cycle of the carrier after data analysis and factorial fitting, as shown in equations (3)–(3).
(3)
I
( t )
= I
( 0 )
∑ i
α i e
− t / τ

I(t) is the fluorescence photon distribution detected at a specific time, I (0) is the photon distribution initially observed, (αi) is the preexponential factor, and (τ) is the fluorescence half-life. The order and period of light emission can be obtained by formula calculation. Because most of the optical properties originate from the support, we can judge the change of support after synthesis. It is also chosen as a standard in analytical science.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)
ce:section.ce:para: According to time-resolved photoluminescence, the shortest average emitting half-life of red carbon quantum dots is observed. The average emitting half-life decreases with increasing particle radius due to the energy gap reduction. The core's sp2 mixed resonance π region can be cut into several independent sp2 resonance clusters, absorbing the fundamental luminescence energy and transmitting it to the surface and defects. More than half of the surface structure of carbon quantum dots is sp3 hybrid and part is sp2 hybrid. The surface has many functional groups, mainly hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups, which have various energy levels so that the functional groups can be used as emission traps. Part of the sp2 hybrid region is also an emission trap. In addition, the π and π∗ energy levels of surface sp2 are located between the s and s∗ energy levels of sp3. The defect is a nonperfect sp2 mixing region, emitting energy and producing a nonradiative emitting mode [37,38]. Robertson et al. [39] theoretically calculated that the excitation energy is absorbed by the resonant clusters and released to the surface and defects. Ding et al. [40] successfully shifted the emission from the blue region to the red region by modulating the wavelength of the emission through surface oxidation. Because the number of functional groups on the surface is much larger than the number of defects, the surface energy level is adjustable. It is the main emitting energy level of carbon quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 3b. The surface of carbon quantum dots is an amorphous structure, so the electron holes can quickly release energy through radiation mitigation and carry out photocatalytic reactions to emit electrons. The environment soon affects the highly reactive surface, such as oxygen and water in the air, ions in an aqueous solution, dust in the container, etc. When the surface is damaged, not only is the surface changed but also the surface is damaged. When the surface is damaged, it changes the surface energy level and causes carbon quantum dot disintegration. To maintain the stability of quantum dots, passivation agents can be added during material synthesis to effectively prevent environmental damage to the QD surface. Common types are polymers, polyamines, surfactants, and dendrimers.

The synchrotron radiation light source emits electrons through a high-speed electron gun, gives an energizing ring a strong magnetic field to accelerate the electrons, stabilizes the electron movement in the storage ring, and emits continuous electromagnetic waves in the tangential direction, covering X-ray, infrared, visible, ultraviolet and other bands. It has the advantages of wavelength controllability, high light source intensity, high coherence, time pulse, and polarization. With the above benefits, the experimenter can obtain high-resolution experimental results and shorten the measurement time at the synchrotron radiation circle.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry is suitable for analyzing materials in the depth range of 1 nm–10 nm and can detect elemental composition, oxidation state, relative content, and coordination environment. When the material is excited by X-rays, the electrons in the material element's inner orbital region are dissociated and called photoelectrons. In an ideal state, the binding energy can be obtained from energy conservation, as shown in formula (3-4).
(4)
E k
= h υ −
E b
− φ

Ek is the kinetic energy of the photoelectron, hυ is the X-ray energy, h is the Plank constant, υ is the X-ray frequency, Eb is the electron binding energy of the atomic orbital, and φ is the work function. Using the kinetic energy of photoelectrons to infer the binding energy of electrons, the elements can be qualitatively analyzed from data in standard files. If the material has a higher oxidation state or a highly electronegative coordination environment, the shielding effect of the outer electrons on the nucleus can be reduced, increasing the binding energy, and the peaks on the spectrum will shift to higher energies. In contrast, if the material is closer to the elemental state or has a low negative charge coordination environment, the binding energy will decrease, and the peak value will be shifted to lower energy. The principle is shown in Fig. 3c. The sample is attached using carbon glue, foreign contaminants are removed in a high vacuum environment, and high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is achieved with a high-intensity and high-coherence light source. Thus, we can detect the high-resolution XPS spectra at low atomic weight percentages.

When X-rays are incident on the sample to excite the inner electrons, the element's electrons jump to the continuum state to form photoelectrons or to the unoccupied states to produce auger electrons, which are discontinuous according to the selection rule. This phenomenon is called X-light absorption; the main absorption is called the absorption edge. The principal quantum number classifies the absorption edge. The principal quantum numbers (n) are 1, 2, and 3 for the K, L, and M absorption edges, and the total angular momentum quantum number is taken into account to subdivide the p-orbital domain into the 2p 1/2 and 2p 3/2 orbital domains, which are the L2 and L3 absorption edges. Based on the absorption edge energy, −20 eV to +50 eV is called X-ray absorption near-edge structure, and +50 eV to +1000 eV is the extended X-ray absorption fine structure. The principle of backscattering, which helps to deduce the interaction between the atom under test and the neighboring atoms, is shown in Fig. 3d. The X-ray absorption near-edge structure spectrum can be used to analyze the local coordination environment, average oxidation number, electronic structure, and other relevant information. We can realize average bond length, the coordination number of neighboring atoms, and atomic ordering via the X-ray absorption fine structure. XAS is mainly measured in transmission mode or fluorescence yield mode. The transmission mode is suitable for highly concentrated samples and should be formulated to avoid self-absorption. The relationship between I0 and I is shown in equations (3)–(5) by measuring the incident X-ray intensity (I0) and the X-ray intensity (I) after penetrating the sample, with the sample thickness as x and the absorption coefficient as μ.
(5)
I =
I 0
e
− μ x
(5a)
I f
/
I 0
∝ μ

The fluorescence mode is suitable for low-concentration samples or samples with low light penetration. The relationship between the incident X-ray intensity I0 and the fluorescence intensity If of the model with the absorption coefficient μ is shown in the equation. XAS is the most powerful technique to define the pure phase single-atom structure. It is important to compare vibration and characteristic peaks between standards and attempted samples. We will know coordination numbers and types of neighboring atoms when we observed the high similar vibration between samples at near edge position. In the R-space diagram, due to the varied back-scattering, the characteristic peaks are related to bonding modes. Then, we can assign the peaks easily after scanning standards.

As the dimensions of materials become smaller, quantum effects become more important and give rise to many nanoclusters with unique atomic and electronic structures, magnetism, mechanics, and optics that differ from their properties as solid materials, and other physical properties. In addition, quantum effects cause many nanoclusters to have unique catalytic effects and chemical activities. The remarkable physical and chemical properties of these nanoclusters are closely related to their electron energy distribution, spin configuration, and geometric structure. The attempted catalytic property of SANs is highly dependent on the active sites of the metal. By designing properly coordinated environments, we can stabilize the selected metal atoms and give them a particular valence state. Therefore, the active sites are well known for promoting photocatalysis, photon-electron conversion, and photothermal enhancement. SANs have many advantages, including easy separation, recyclability, immobilization, uniform active sites, tunable coordination environment, and high atom utilization. Therefore, SANs are expected to become a bridge between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis. At the same time, SANs also provide opportunities for revealing the catalytic structure-activity relationship and studying the reaction mechanism at the atomic level. Since the catalytic activities and selectivities of the SANs are different from those of their corresponding metal surfaces, an increasing number of SANs have been developed for electrocatalysis, thermocatalysis, photocatalysis, energy storage, and organic electrochemical synthesis. The types of metals, active sites, catalytic properties, and applications are listed in Table 1 . Carbon-based SANs such as M-N-C (M = Fe, Zn, Mn, Co, etc.) have M-Nx sites similar to those of natural enzymes, especially in terms of chemical, geometric and electronic structures that can be regarded as catalytic pockets identical to those of natural enzymes. In addition, some SANs with other active centers are the noble metals on the periodic table, such as single atomic Pt, Co, and Cu (see Table 1). They contain coordination sites that bind effectively to carbon nanostructures rich in functional groups by simple physisorption, making them noble metal-carried SANs. In biomedicine, high efficiency and low risk are the goals to be pursued; therefore, the application of SANs to biomedicine is gaining attention. The design of SANs depends on the biotoxicity of the carrier and the metal. Both of them must be biocompatible materials [41]. The main types of monoatomic enzymes can be classified as biosensors, oxidative-press cytoprotection, bacterial disinfection, and cancer treatment, as shown in Fig. 1.

In recent years, high sensitivity, strong selectivity, and improved detection limits have attracted many scientists. Although nanoparticles show good activity in this field, some unavoidable side reactions depress the details of sample signals. According to the active atomic sites and better catalytic properties of SANs, they have been developed to solve these problems. Qin et al. designed a photoelectron platform by combining well-defined single-atom iron and Cu2O/Ti3C2Tx. Due to the single-atom iron, the interfacial oxygen reduction reaction is amplified to decrease the effect of the POD-like reaction [59]. The limit of detection (LOD) of the platform reached 0.22 mU/mL in acetylcholinesterase activity detection and was further applied to organophosphorus pesticides (Fig. 4 a). It remained linear in the range of 0.5–600 ng/mL and the precipitation could be observed easily. Bushira et al. constructed an Fe–N5-based single-atom biosensor. Owing to the electrocatalytic property of SANs under neutral conditions, rapid response and high sensitivity are achieved. In addition, there was no interference between the simultaneous examination of dopamine and uric acid in the human serum [63]. The dopamine and uric acid can be tracked from 0.005 to 500 μM and from 0.01 to 480 μM in a reliable linear range, respectively (Fig. 4b). Zeng et al. [92] anchored atomic platinum on hollow cadmium sulfide spheres to cooperate with enzyme-encapsulated DNA flowers. The team adopted CD63 aptamers as the biorecognition protein of the biosensor. The efficiency of photon-electron conversion of single-atom Pt is 1.5 times that of Pt nanoparticles during light on-off tests. In HepG2 cell-derived exosome detection, the interference of glucose was eliminated by glucose oxidase.

Therefore, the photocurrent was valid when the concentration of exosomes increased from 0 to 50,000 particles/μL (Fig. 4c). Ge et al. [60] designed an N-doped carbon-based single-atom iron-type colorimetric malathion paper assay as shown in Fig. 4d. The iron atom oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in a hydrogen peroxide environment through an OXD-like reaction, and the sensor presented a blue color. Due to the acid, phosphatase can be hydrolyzed to produce ascorbic acid, which shows reductive ability. In the report, malathion inhibited hydrolysis and changed the color, which can be quantified by smartphone in the test. The concentration of malathion was available with an LOD of 0.42 nM.

POD-like mimicking enzymes are the most popular in the biosensing field. Hydroxyl radicals can oxidize substrates via the reaction. Lyu et al. [69] synthesized single-atom iron on an N-doped carbon via the ion-imprinting method. The concentration of H2O2 was quantified by POD-like catalysis. The research team revealed that the SANs maintained their activity and selectivity in a pH range of 2.5–8.5 and with an increase in temperature from 4 to 80 °C. In the H2O2 detection of MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells, the curve showed a linear trend from 0.25 to 5 mM. The high biocompatibility was manifested by increasing the concentration of SANs from 1.0 to 10 μg/mL (Fig. 5 a). Wu et al. [77], stabilized copper atoms on N-doped carbon and participated in the acetylcholinesterase and choline system. The hydroxyl radical generated by copper atoms can be indicated by TMB. Therefore, the detection of acetylcholinesterase shows a linear relationship with concentrations from 10 to 8000 μM. Furthermore, the probe was applied to organophosphorus pesticide tracing by controlling the activity of acetylcholinesterase. The higher the concentration of organophosphorus pesticides was, the lower the absorbance peak at 652 nm of TMB (Fig. 5b). Ouyang et al. [81] dispersed cobalt atoms on the SiO2 yolk shell. The POD-like system was further modified for the chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA) of the AFB1 antibody. The linear range was suitable for 10–1000 pg/mL, and the LOD was 0.44 pg/mL. CLIA was applied to the AFB1 antibody of G. uralensis and P. notoginseng, and the recovery rates were higher than 90%. In addition, the test and standard deviation of CLIA satisfied the comment of commercial ELISA (Fig. 5c). Xu et al. [62] assembled the single-atom iron-based sandwich-type linked immunosorbent assay. The axial site nitrogen enforced the interaction between iron atoms and intermediates. Due to the strong catalytic power, the absorbance of labeled carcinoembryonic antigen increased linearly in the range of 1–1000 pg/mL with an LOD of 0.55 pg/mL. This proved that the assay worked at a wider concentration region than commercial ELISA (Fig. 5d). Moreover, Cai et al. [61] chose lateral flow immunoassay (LIFA) to demonstrate the POD-like activity of single-atom iron nanoenzymes. LIFA was used for aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and fumonisin B1 (FB1) with the addition of a chromogenic solution. The LODs of AFB1 and FB1 were 2.8 and 13.9 pg/mL, respectively. Owing to the chromogenic solution, the relative concentrations of AFB1 and FB1 can be compared by eye or smartphone. Finally, LIFA has high selectivity for other types of mycotoxins. The scientists reported that LIFA contributed to food contaminant evaluation (Fig. 5e). Due to their robust activity, SANs successfully resolved the limit of conventional biosensing methods. Their high selectivity gave us more details about our samples. Consequently, they are suitable for many examinations, including ingredient detection and health care.

Radicals are harmful to live cells. They boost the rate of cell apoptosis, dysfunction, and deterioration in our bodies. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) are the most popular due to their frequent appearance in cell metabolism. CAT and SOD were the most efficient enzymes to prevent the radicals generated from H2O2 or NO3 −. Therefore, we can use this concept to design CAT-like and SOD-like SANs to protect our cells. Afterward, the SANs assisted in the healing process. Ma et al. anchored iron atoms on N-doped porous carbon to mimic SOD and CAT [93]. The HeLa cells were treated with 10 mM β-lapachone for 6 h to stimulate ROS. After the HeLa cells were treated with 30 mg/mL SANs, the cell viability was more than 85%. The fluorescence of the cells in the SANsgroup was 6.8-fold lower than that of the β-lapachone-treated HeLa cells (Fig. 6 a). Cao et al. set the cobalt atoms on porphyrin centers of carbon materials. The CAT-like, SOD-like, and GPx-like properties of Co-SANs could eliminate ROS and RNS [78]. The research team applied the materials to sepsis therapy. The Co-SANs were introduced to lipopolysaccharide-induced sepsis. It was shown that approximately 60–80% of ROS were scavenged as well as RNS. The levels of proinflammatory cytokines were reduced to 10% after 14 days of treatment. Similar phenomena were also found in the mouse models arising from Escherichia coli (Fig. 6b). Zhang et al. [42] constructed N-doped carbon to support Fe–Cu, Rh, and V. They compared the catalytic properties of three types of SANs. Fe–Cu showed SOD-like and CAT-like abilities. V preferred GPx-like and POD-like properties. Rh was prone to POD-like and CAT-like features. The group injected these SANs into mice with brain trauma. The wounds recovered within 9 days in all SANs groups due to the inhibition of oxidative stress in the scalps. Interestingly, as indicated by the CD31 marker, V and Fe–Cu were observed to have more neovascularization and VEGF enhancement than Rh. Biocompatibility was proven by detecting no metal leakage (Fig. 6c). SANs are good at repairing cell defects. The low-weight percent of metal would not negatively influence the cells.

Bacteria are a high concern for everyone during the period of wound recovery. Inflammatory and low healing rates are problematic if the bacteria infect the wound. Scar shrinkage is also a goal to achieve. Traditional treatments only prevent the injury from irritations and show weak antibacterial effects. Nanoparticles are famous for good catalysis, but scientists doubt the potential risks of metal toxicity. Huo et al. [45] put single iron atoms in N-doped amorphous carbon to evaluate POD-like and PTT ability. Iron SANs (250 μg/mL) were injected into E. coli- and S. aureus-infected mice. The localized temperature climbed from 32.5 to 49 °C after being exposed to an 808 nm infrared laser for 10 min. In addition, 58.3% of the relative wound closure area and granulation tissue growth were achieved by the cooperation of POD-like catalysis and PTT enhancement. In contrast, only a 40% recovery area was found in the control group with the emergence of extravasating neutrophils (Fig. 7 a). Surface modification was adopted to strengthen the power of single-atom iron catalysts. Yang et al. coated iron single-atom catalysts with phenylboronic acid group-functionalized carboxylated chitosan to improve hydrophilicity [43]. The modified SA enzyme presented POD-like, OXD-like, and CAT-like properties and was administered to injured mice at 25 μg/mL. The regional temperature increased to 55 °C within 5 min. The wound area decreased to approximately 22.1% after the treatments, which was better than the result of the vancomycin method. Moreover, scientists declared the biocompatibility of modified iron SANs by the average level of white blood cells in the blood test (Fig. 7b). Other metal atoms could be made as single-atom catalysts. Wang et al. [73] dispersed copper atoms on N-doped porous carbon. Copper showed POD-like, GSH peroxidase-like, and photothermal properties. The wound is infected by methicillin-resistant S. aureus in animal models. Cu SA enzyme and H2O2 were 100 μg/mL and 100 μM, respectively. The wound was exposed to an 808 nm NIR laser for 2 min. The temperature was 45 °C to ensure the best catalytic effect. No inflammation was found in the therapy. Then, the synergistic effect promoted the growth of a new epidermal layer. There was no apparent damage to organs or the blood system in H&E staining and blood examination (Fig. 7c). Feng et al. [71] combined oxygen-coordinated single tungsten atoms with cotton fabrics through direct-dye processing. The manifested cotton was attached to the S. aureus-infected wound. Compared to raw cotton fabrics, the SA-cotton fabrics presented a more substantial healing effect and depressed most bacteria via the halo POD-like properties. Additionally, the SA-cotton fabrics can be soap-washed for approximately 20 cycles, which means they have stronger water resistance than traditional materials. Therefore, attenuating the potential toxicity of metal leakage was achieved (Fig. 7d). Single-atom tungsten materials were applied to element collection while killing the bacteria. Wang et al. [70] tried a metal-organic framework as the support of tungsten atoms. The tungsten atoms not only possess POD-like properties but also capture uranium. Comparing the growing situation with the control group, W-SANs suppress 92.0% for E. coli and 94.4% for S. aureus. Furthermore, W-SANs produced hypobromous acid from seawater, which enhanced reusability for uranium capture. After five regeneration cycles, the W-SA enzyme maintained 78.0% of the original uranium extraction capacity, which was 6.8 times that of the metal organic framework. Therefore, we know that the anti-biofouling ability shows good durability (Fig. 7e).

Innovation in cancer treatments is of great concern in the single-atom research field. Considering the biosafety issue, iron is widely adopted as the active site of single-atom nanomedicine. Interestingly, scientists are devoted to lowering the risk of other metals demonstrating more substantial catalytic potential. Since the support materials of SANs are mostly carbon quantum dots. By loading different monatomic metals, SANs are considered candidates for phototherapy. Phototherapy is divided into PTT and PDT. PTT is when the carrier absorbs a light source of a specific wavelength through the electronic resonance of the metal surface, and the thermal energy that overflows to the vicinity is generated to achieve the purpose of treatment. PDT uses light to irradiate the local environment to generate ROS for treatment. This therapy requires photosensitive substances, excitation light sources, and oxygen-containing molecules. The energy effect generated by photothermal heat can reach more than 50 nm, while singlet oxygen only affects the surrounding environment where it is generated, usually within a 20 nm radius. Due to the size of CDs below 20 nm, many accumulated SANs can provide more effective light efficiency than single nanoparticles. In addition, since the support materials of SANs are all inorganic material components, they can be stably and continuously used as long-acting photosensitizers. Consequently, it is essential to discuss the effect of these SANs. Zhu et al. [74] fixed copper atoms on mesoporous carbon spheres. Then, they encapsulated pantoprazole sodium and Cu-SANs with membrane vesicles. The author introduced 10 mg/kg nanomedicine to compete with MGC803. The nanomedicine showed a 5-fold targeting affinity compared to raw membrane vesicles. Because of the synergistic effect of the proton pump inhibitor and the POD-like property of Cu, glutathione was downregulated. Hence, nearly 90% of the tumor volume was shrunk by nanomedicine. This study provides a new method to target CD44-positive gastric cancer. Cobalt has also been seen as a toxic metal. Huang et al. [76] constructed a tumor exosome bionic SANs/aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) cascade catalysis system to enhance center dot OH generation and facilitate efficient tumor penetration. The Cu SANs were prepared with peroxidase activity, followed by coating with AIEgens absorbed tumor-derived exosomes to construct the cascade catalysis system. The H2O2 interacts with the Cu SANs, producing center dot OH in the tumor. Wang et al. [79] pyrolyzed the cobalt and TiO2 precursors under atmospheric conditions to enhance the percentage of cobalt (II). The cobalt atoms displayed CAT-like and OXD-like activities. Later, the biocompatibility of SANs was improved by polyethylene glycol. TiO2 is a well-known photoelectrocatalyst. In the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse experiment, the Co-SA enzyme loaded with β-lapachone emitted the most robust oxidative stress fluorescence. The slope of the hybrid nanosystem was the gentlest in the size tracking diagram. The drastic necrosis-treated tumor slices were found by staining methods that demonstrated the synergistic effect of drugs and the Co-SANs. The biosafety issue was solved by supplying the high cell viability of normal cells (HUVECs), body weight, and blood tests. Heavy transition metals usually showed intense activities. Wang et al. well-dispersed ruthenium atoms on carbon dots. The Ru-SA enzyme possessed OXD-like, POD-like, and glutathione oxidase-like abilities. The 4T1 cell-transplanted mice were injected with 100 μL of 1000 μg/mL Ru-SANs. Owing to the high photothermal efficiency, the body temperature of the mice reached 54.0 °C after heating with an 808 nm laser for 5 min. Furthermore, the authors found a synergetic effect of photothermal and catalytic properties. The Ru-SANs destroyed the tumor surface. However, with laser irradiation, the tumor showed a smaller volume and deeper necrosis level. Some of the popular safe metals were designed as active sites. Xing et al. [84] synthesized carbon-based flower-like zinc-SANs to enhance the surface area. The Zn-SANs presented POD-like activity. Then, doxorubicin was loaded onto the SANs before in vivo tests. MCF-7/ADR tumor-carrying mice were treated with 3 mg/kg nanomedicine doses. The nanosystems were allowed to accumulate in the tumor for 24 h. The tumor inhibition rate was 85.4%, and the nanosystems produced the most significant apoptosis region. Zhu et al. [88] constructed polyethylene glycol-modified Mn-SANs to enhance Fenton-like reactions. In addition, the nanomedicine exhibited excellent NIR conversion. According to the 4T1 tumor mouse experiments, the temperature of the tumor site increased to 51.98 °C in 5 min. The mice were injected with a dose of 15 mg/kg nanomedicine and subjected to 10 min of NIR laser treatment. Compared with the control group, a noticeable diminishing of the tumor was discovered. A large apoptosis region was found in the staining images after 14 days of treatment. The novel concept of support was essential to enforce the abilities of SANs. Du et al. [90] claimed that single-atom palladium can be stabilized by nitrogen and chloride atoms through the self-assembly method. B16F10 cells were transplanted into the mice. The therapy included ultrasonic and 652 nm laser irradiation after injection with Pd-SANs. The photo/sono-sensitizer behaved well with single-atom Pd to generate abundant ROS. The therapeutic process represented 90% inhibition of tumor growth and the most dead cells. Additionally, the morphology of the tumor can be checked by ultrasonic imaging. H&E staining showed no apparent defects in the organ slices. Iron is the most prevalent metal in the world. It is necessary for the operation of our hemoglobin. Iron is also a necessary cofactor for natural enzymes, including metabolism and DNA repair. To elevate performance, single-atom iron has been developed by scientists. Catalytic properties and low toxicity are the critical features of iron. Thus, it is widely manifested in nanomedicine in cancer therapy. Qin et al. [47] attached iron atoms to the defects of carbon dots. Then, the nanosystems were confined to spherical mesoporous silica. The active iron sites depleted the glutathione concentration to 60%. In addition, after being exposed to an 808 nm laser, the Fe-SANs improved their inherited Fenton-like reaction efficiency. In the SMMC-7721 bearing mouse test, the temperature increased by 17.4 °C, and the cell apoptosis rate reached 60.7% in the Fe-SANs + laser group. The hepatic and renal assays confirmed the biocompatibility (Fig. 8 a). Interestingly, Muhammad et al. [51] chose the carbon dots as the support for iron atoms as well. They claimed that the Fe-SANs mimic OXD, CAT, SOD, POD, glutathione peroxidase, and thiol peroxidase. Subsequently, Fe-SANs were linked with angiopep-2 (Ang) to promote transcytosis capacity and glioblastoma accumulation. Orthotopic U87MG-Luc and U251-TR tumor-bearing mice were used as the experimental models. After intravenous tail injections, Fe-SANs-Ang showed the strongest inhibition ability. An intense fluorescence was detected, indicating excellent blood-brain barrier penetration. The accumulation was approximately 4.5% of the injected dosage/gram, and the survival time was approximately 50 days. Fe-SAN-Ang has negligible toxicity to the liver and kidney, and a large amount of Fe can be excreted by urine (Fig. 8b). Wang et al. [53] decided to use collaborative single iron atoms with bioglass scaffolds. The SAN scaffolds were applied to the osteosarcoma treatment, during which scattered bacterial distribution of sagittal and coronal sections of bone were captured by computed tomography. For anticancer activity, the SAN scaffolds showed better NIR-to-heat conversion (33–58 °C) than the primitive scaffolds. The tumor volume was smaller than 100 mm3 and the survival period increased. Moreover, the recovery rate reached 94.3%. SAN scaffolds were combined with neo-formed bone tissue at the edge of the defect. The properties boosted multiple protein attachment, fibroblast migration, and bone marrow mesenchymal stem cell differentiation (Fig. 8c). NIR-II is considered a mild photothermal resource in biochemistry. Qi et al. [49] developed platelet membrane-coated single-atom iron nanoparticles. The Fe-SANs destroyed mitochondria in tumors and were located in the NIR-II window. Following the JC-1 probing result, the JC monomer was released, indicating collapsed mitochondria. In the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse test, an obvious temperature increase was observed in the Fe-SAN group after exposure to a 1064 nm laser. The tumor volume and weight decreased gradually over a period of 2 weeks.

Through immunoassays, the author verified that Fe-SANs + NIR caused severe tumor cell apoptosis (Fig. 8d). Aptamers and DNA were nominated as new targeting molecules in cancer therapies. Cao et al. [52] developed aptamer and DNA-covered single atom-iron material. Due to the aptamers and DNA, the Fe-SANs could enhance the affinity between catalytic substrates, such as H2O2 and glutathione. Fe-SANs were used to treat MCF-7 and CT-26 mice. The Fe-SANs inhibited the growth rate of both cancers. The staining images implied nuclear fragmentation and morphology destruction. The expression of glutathione can be judged by glutathione peroxidase 4. Compared with the control group, glutathione peroxidase 4 was inactivated by Fe-SANs (Fig. 8e). Interestingly, the neighborhood environment was highly related to the average oxidative states of the metal atoms. Tuning the coordination atoms would be critical to upgrading the SANs. Ji et al. [54] announced that 1 P atom and 3 N atoms can stabilize the iron atom. The FeP1N3-SANs generated a 10-fold higher peroxide concentration than the FeN4-type SANs. Additionally, FeP1N3-SANs were better than horseradish peroxidase. FeP1N3-SANs (50 μg/mL) were injected intratumorally into HepG2 tumor-carrying mice. After 14 days of therapy, the tumor size was 40.6% of that of the control group. However, without targeting molecules, the tumor volume remained 87.9% of the control group while choosing intravenous injection. No abnormal organs or tissues were discovered when the concentration was elevated to 20 mg/kg (Fig. 8f). In the field of cancer treatments, a variety of metals have been examined. At the atomic scale, metals maintained their inherent catalytic properties and reduced the risk of toxicity. SANs are proven to be efficient and involved with various catalytic properties. They broaden the application of conventional nanomedicine. More thorough clinical investigations must be completed, which would allow general acceptance of the SANs.

SANs are a new concept in multiphase catalysis, where the homogeneous active site of atomic dispersion not only maximizes the utilization of metal atoms but also has the potential to bridge the gap between multiphase catalysis and homogeneous catalysis. SANs utilize inorganic materials as support materials and can carry polymers or biofilm structures through surface modification to promote dispersibility and biocompatibility. They can also be modified to link biomolecular signals or drugs, allowing SANs to target materials and drugs to accumulate specifically in specific tissues, such as tumor cells or bacteria. Based on the current research directions, four concepts for future development can be identified: (1) SANs emphasize the homogeneity of dispersion. The catalytic activity of each metal single atom may be different due to the existence of surface heterogeneity of the carrier, i.e., each metal atom is in a different chemical environment, and it is worth exploring which metal single atoms in the chemical environment play a “one-for-one” role. (2) Atomically dispersed supported metal catalysts (ADMCs) emphasize 100% dispersion, with possibly atom, cluster, and two-dimensional single-atom layers (each atom is in direct contact with the carrier and can extend indefinitely in the horizontal direction, with only one layer of atoms in the vertical direction). This concept is broader and more objective, and is a universal term. (3) Single-site heterogeneous catalysts (SSCs) emphasize the singularity of the sites, with no requirement for the morphology of the sites. Each site must be the same, and the activity is the same, derived from homogeneous catalysis, where each molecule of the same homogeneous catalyst is indistinguishable. (4) Site-isolated heterogeneous catalyst (IHC) emphasizes isolation; the single site is indistinguishable from the isolated site. It can be understood that the study of single-atomic level dispersion catalysts has become the new norm in the field of catalysis, no matter which direction it goes. Nevertheless, there seems to be much potential to resolve the ligand structures and active sites in monoatomic-level dispersion catalysts at the atomic level. Despite the leap forward in biosensing and biomedical applications, there are still many challenges in the future applications of SANs. The first is that fluorescent and electrochemical signals for tracking mostly do the signal detection of SANs. Due to the complexity of the composition of living organisms, the blockage and limitation of the optical spectrum and charge distribution become the reason why SANs cannot be effectively detected and distinguished. Secondly, biosafety is an important issue. Simple support materials such as carbon dots or silica nanoparticles may not cause significant clinical toxicity. Still, the long-term accumulation of noble metal elements on SANs may cause adverse pharmacokinetic effects on the body. The long-term effects of SANs should be tested in animal models before their use in clinical translations. To optimize the experimental conditions to realize the possibility of the future layout of SANs in clinical trials.
